results.question: Do genetically identical cells express the same gene at the same level?

hyp_council.question: Do genetically identical cells express the same gene at the same level?
hyp_council.claim: Genetically identical E. coli cells exhibit cell-to-cell variation in gene expression levels due to intrinsic stochastic noise, such that the coefficient of variation (CV) of protein abundance across isogenic cells under identical conditions is ≥0.15 for at least 50% of genes.
hyp_council.h1: Isogenic E. coli cells grown under identical conditions show substantial heterogeneity in single-gene protein expression levels, with the majority of genes displaying CV ≥ 0.15 across independent stochastic replicates (seeds).
hyp_council.h0: Genetically identical cells under identical conditions express each gene at the same level (CV ≈ 0, within numerical precision); any observed variation is negligible.

operational_defs.measure: steady-state protein copy number (counts) for each gene, sampled across seeds at generation 10

operational_defs.observable: coefficient of variation (CV = std / mean) of protein[GENE_ID] across seeds
operational_defs.measure: CV computed over 50 independent stochastic replicates (seeds) for each gene
same genome, same condition, only stochastic seed differs
per-gene protein count normalized by total protein mass (fg) for each seed, to isolate intrinsic from extrinsic noise

hyp_council.predicted_effect: CV ≥ 0.15 for ≥50% of assayed genes. Predicted distribution: bimodal or right-skewed, with low-abundance genes (mean < 100 copies) showing CV ~0.2–0.5, and high-abundance genes showing CV ~0.1–0.2. After normalization by protein_mass, CV remains ≥0.15 for ≥50% of genes (indicating intrinsic noise dominates).

falsifier.decision_rule: For each of 20 pre-specified genes (rpoB, rpoC, rpoD, rpsA, rpsB, rplA, rplB, tufA, tufB, groEL, dnaK, lacZ, acrB, ompF, malE, araC, crp, fis, hns, lrp) spanning low/medium/high mean abundance: (1) compute CV_raw = std(protein[GENE_ID]) / mean(protein[GENE_ID]) across 50 seeds at generation 10; (2) compute CV_norm = std(protein[GENE_ID]/protein_mass) / mean(protein[GENE_ID]/protein_mass) across the same seeds; (3) classify gene as 'intrinsically variable' if CV_raw ≥ 0.15. Reject H1 if fewer than 50% of the 20 genes are classified as intrinsically variable (one-sample binomial test against proportion = 0.5, α = 0.05).
falsifier.refuting_result: If <10 of 20 genes (i.e., <50%) show CV_raw ≥ 0.15, H1 is refuted and we retain H0 (negligible intrinsic noise).

rivals.claim: Apparent variation is due to extrinsic (global) factors—differences in cell size, growth rate, or ribosome content—rather than gene-specific intrinsic noise. Normalizing protein counts by total protein mass collapses the variation.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If variation is extrinsic, then CV_norm (protein[GENE_ID]/protein_mass) will drop to ≤0.05 for >80% of the 20 genes. If CV_norm remains ≥0.15 for ≥50% of genes, intrinsic noise dominates and H1 is supported over this rival.

rivals.claim: Variation arises from deterministic chaos (sensitive dependence on initial conditions in a deterministic model) rather than stochastic gene expression noise.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If the model is deterministic, identical seeds yield identical trajectories; CV across seeds with the same seed value = 0. If CV > 0 across distinct seeds, stochasticity is present. (This is a simulator-architecture question; we assume stochasticity is implemented and record determinism as an auxiliary assumption.)

auxiliary_assumptions: The simulator implements stochastic gene expression (e.g. Gillespie-style or Langevin transcription/translation noise); it is not purely deterministic.
Seeds are truly independent draws from the stochastic process, not correlated pseudo-random streams.
Generation 10 is sufficient for the system to reach a steady-state distribution of protein levels.
The model computes per-gene protein counts (protein[GENE_ID] for each gene) and these are accessible as named species.

The 20 pre-specified genes (rpoB, rpoC, rpoD, rpsA, rpsB, rplA, rplB, tufA, tufB, groEL, dnaK, lacZ, acrB, ompF, malE, araC, crp, fis, hns, lrp) provide adequate coverage of the genome's expression-level distribution (low/medium/high abundance) and include housekeeping, regulatory, and inducible genes.
CV ≥ 0.15 is a biologically meaningful threshold for 'substantial' noise, consistent with single-cell literature (Elowitz et al. 2002; Taniguchi et al. 2010).
The channel 'protein_mass' refers to total protein mass per cell (fg), which is recorded for each seed alongside per-gene protein counts, enabling normalization.
If the simulator does not expose per-gene protein readouts (protein[GENE_ID]), the test cannot be executed; this is a known instrument dependency.

hyp_agent.question: Do genetically identical cells express the same gene at the same level?
hyp_agent.claim: Genetically identical E. coli cells growing in the same static environment exhibit substantial cell-to-cell variation in the expression level of individual genes, with the coefficient of variation (CV) in protein copy number typically exceeding 0.2 (20%) for most genes due to intrinsic stochastic fluctuations in transcription and translation.
hyp_agent.h1: Intrinsic stochastic noise in gene expression causes genetically identical cells under identical conditions to display heterogeneous protein levels, with CV > 0.2 for a majority of genes.
hyp_agent.h0: Genetically identical cells in the same environment express genes at uniform levels; any observed variation is negligible (CV ≤ 0.05).

operational_defs.measure: mean copy number of a named protein species across stochastic replicates (seeds) at steady state

operational_defs.observable: coefficient of variation (CV) in protein copy number
operational_defs.measure: standard deviation / mean of protein copy number across seeds for a given protein species
multiple seeds (≥30) of the wildtype/basal design, each representing one cell

hyp_agent.predicted_effect: CV in protein copy number will exceed 0.2 (20%) for the majority (>50%) of surveyed genes. For highly expressed constitutive genes the CV may be lower (~0.1–0.15), while for low-copy or bursty genes the CV may reach 0.4–0.8. The distribution of CV across genes is predicted to be right-skewed with median CV ≈ 0.25.

falsifier.channel: protein copy number distribution across seeds for a panel of ≥10 representative genes (high/medium/low expression)
falsifier.decision_rule: For each of ≥10 surveyed genes, compute CV = σ/μ of protein copy number across ≥30 seeds at steady state (generation 10). Reject H1 if fewer than 50% of genes show CV > 0.2, or if the median CV across all genes is ≤ 0.1.
falsifier.refuting_result: Fewer than 50% of surveyed genes have CV > 0.2, or median CV ≤ 0.1, indicating negligible cell-to-cell variation.

rivals.claim: Observed variation is due to extrinsic noise (global cellular state fluctuations such as ribosome or resource availability) rather than intrinsic gene-specific stochasticity.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If extrinsic noise dominates, protein levels of different genes within the same cell should be strongly correlated across seeds. Measuring pairwise Pearson correlation of protein copy numbers for multiple gene pairs across seeds would yield high correlations (r > 0.7). In contrast, intrinsic noise predicts weak or no correlation (r < 0.3) between unrelated genes.

rivals.claim: Variation arises from differences in cell-cycle phase or physiological state (e.g., mass, growth rate) rather than stochastic gene expression per se.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If cell-cycle or physiological state is the source, normalizing protein copy number by a global reporter (e.g., dry_mass or ribosome_conc) would collapse the variation (CV of normalized values ≤ 0.1). If intrinsic noise dominates, normalization will not substantially reduce CV (CV remains > 0.15).

auxiliary_assumptions: The simulator's stochastic engine faithfully represents intrinsic transcriptional and translational noise at the single-cell level.
Each seed represents an independent cell at steady state; transient dynamics have equilibrated by generation 10.
The basal condition is a well-mixed, constant environment with no spatial or temporal gradients.
The model computes protein copy numbers for individual species and these are accessible as named protein species readouts.
The panel of surveyed genes spans a range of expression levels and regulatory modes (constitutive, regulated, high-copy, low-copy) to be representative of the genome.
Any variation due to numerical solver noise is negligible compared to biological stochasticity.

results.brief_council:
## Thesis
This operationalization is correct because it directly measures protein-level variance across isogenic replicates—the only empirical signature of stochastic gene expression—using the instrument's native stochasticity mechanism (seeds) and discriminates intrinsic from extrinsic noise through protein_mass normalization, thereby capturing the biological essence of "same gene, same level" as a distributional claim rather than a point estimate.

## Why this operationalization is faithful to the question

**"Genetically identical cells"** → `wildtype/basal` with varying `seeds`  
The instrument generates isogenic populations by fixing genome and condition; `seeds` vary only the stochastic realization. This is the computational analogue of clonal bacteria in microfluidic chambers.

**"Same gene"** → `protein[GENE_ID]` for 20 pre-specified loci  
The instrument exposes per-gene protein counts as named species. Selecting 20 genes (rpoB, rpoC, rpoD, rpsA, rpsB, rplA, rplB, tufA, tufB, groEL, dnaK, lacZ, acrB, ompF, malE, araC, crp, fis, hns, lrp) spanning housekeeping, regulatory, and inducible functions ensures the test is not confined to atypical edge-cases but samples the expression-level distribution (low/medium/high abundance).

**"Same level"** → CV of `protein[GENE_ID]` across seeds  
The null hypothesis interprets "same level" as deterministic identity (CV ≈ 0). The alternative interprets it as empirical variance (CV ≥ 0.15), consistent with single-cell measurements. The question is inherently about whether a *distribution* collapses to a point or retains spread; CV is the natural statistic for comparing spread relative to mean.

**"Express"** → steady-state protein copy number at generation 10  
Gene expression level operationalizes to the downstream protein product (the functional output), not mRNA alone. Generation 10 ensures transient effects from initialization have dissipated and the system reflects steady-state stochastic equilibrium.

**Extrinsic vs. intrinsic discrimination** → normalization by `protein_mass`  
The raw question is ambiguous about whether "same level" means absolute counts or relative expression. Normalizing by total protein mass (a proxy for cell size/global state) isolates gene-specific stochasticity from correlated fluctuations in ribosome number, growth rate, or division timing. This directly addresses whether variation is *intrinsic to the gene* or a consequence of whole-cell extrinsic variation.

**No smuggled assumptions:**  
The operationalization does not presuppose the *magnitude* of noise (the threshold 0.15 is stated as a falsifiable claim, not assumed), nor does it select genes post-hoc. The 20-gene panel is fixed a priori. The use of CV (rather than variance) prevents confounding abundance with noisiness.

## Why it is decisive

**Falsifiability:**  
H₀ ("CV ≈ 0") is rejected if ≥10 of 20 genes show CV ≥ 0.15. This is a one-sample binomial test (proportion > 0.5, α = 0.05), which has exact Type I error control. The null is *falsifiable* because it predicts a specific outcome (near-zero CV for all genes) that the measurement can directly contradict.

**Discriminating power against Rival 1 (extrinsic noise):**  
If variation were purely extrinsic (e.g., cell-size differences driving proportional changes in all proteins), then `protein[GENE_ID]/protein_mass` would collapse to near-constant values (CV_norm ≤ 0.05 for >80% of genes). The operationalization explicitly computes both CV_raw and CV_norm. If CV_norm remains ≥0.15 for ≥50% of genes, this *falsifies* the extrinsic-only hypothesis and supports intrinsic noise. This is a within-design discrimination: the same data adjudicates two rival mechanisms.

**Discriminating power against Rival 2 (deterministic chaos):**  
A deterministic chaotic system would produce identical trajectories for identical seeds. The operationalization uses 50 *distinct* seeds; any CV > 0 across these seeds confirms stochastic noise is implemented in the simulator. This is an auxiliary check (ensuring the instrument matches the assumption), but it is operationally testable: run one gene with 50 seeds vs. the same seed repeated 50 times and verify CV > 0 in the former, CV = 0 in the latter.

**Feasibility:**  
The design requires one perturbation (`wildtype`), one condition (`basal`), 50 seeds, 10 generations, and 20 genes × 2 channels (`protein[GENE_ID]`, `protein_mass`). The instrument schema explicitly supports all these elements. The decision rule is a simple binomial proportion test with no ambiguous cutoffs (the 0.15 threshold is justified by single-cell literature, but the test outcome is insensitive to moderate threshold shifts—CV distributions are typically bimodal or right-skewed, so the 50th percentile is robust). No complex timeline manipulations, no unverified gene knockouts, no reliance on reaction_flux or metabolite species whose simulation fidelity is unknown.

## Why any other operationalization is weaker

**Alternative 1: Measure mRNA instead of protein (`mrna[GENE_ID]`)**  
*Weakness:* mRNA is upstream of the functional output. The question asks about "gene expression level," which biologically refers to the gene's contribution to cellular phenotype—proteins, not transcripts. mRNA noise can be buffered or amplified by translational stochasticity and protein degradation. Operationalizing to mRNA alone would miss post-transcriptional sources of variation (e.g., ribosome binding variability, which is intrinsic noise). Moreover, if CV_mrna were low but CV_protein high, the mRNA operationalization would falsely reject H₁, failing to capture the biological phenomenon the question targets.

**Alternative 2: Use variance instead of CV**  
*Weakness:* Variance scales with mean², confounding abundance with noisiness. A high-abundance housekeeping gene (e.g., rpoB, mean ~1000 copies) could have variance = 10,000 (SD = 100, CV = 0.1) while a low-abundance regulator (mean ~10 copies) has variance = 4 (SD = 2, CV = 0.2). Ranking by variance would spuriously flag high-abundance genes as "variable" even if their *relative* spread is modest. CV is scale-invariant and biologically interpretable (Poisson noise yields CV ∝ 1/√mean; super-Poissonian noise yields CV > 1/√mean). The question asks if cells express "the same gene" at "the same level"—this is a relative comparison within a gene, not across genes, so CV is the faithful statistic.

**Alternative 3: Test all ~1000 genes rather than a pre-specified 20**  
*Weakness:* This introduces multiple-testing burden (requiring Bonferroni or FDR correction, which inflates the false-negative rate) and risks post-hoc selection bias (cherry-picking genes that show noise). More critically, the instrument may not expose `protein[GENE_ID]` for *all* genes (the schema states "species_kinds" include "protein" but does not guarantee per-gene accessibility for the full genome). The 20-gene panel is chosen a priori to span abundance ranges and functional classes, providing a representative sample without assuming exhaustive coverage. If the test were run genome-wide and only 10% of genes showed CV ≥ 0.15, it would still support H₁ as written (majority of genes)—but if those 10% were low-abundance outliers, the claim would be less convincing. The 20-gene approach avoids this by ensuring diversity in the tested set.

**Alternative 4: Use a single seed with many generations (temporal variance)**  
*Weakness:* Temporal variance *within* a lineage conflates intrinsic noise with dynamical fluctuations (e.g., cell-cycle periodicity, metabolic oscillations). The question asks whether "cells" (plural, implying a population) express genes at the same level. A single lineage is one realization of the stochastic process; variance over time in that lineage does not address whether *different cells* are equivalent. The correct test is variance *across* isogenic cells at the *same* timepoint (steady state), which is what seeds provide. Temporal variance would also fail to distinguish extrinsic from intrinsic noise (a single cell experiences both).

**Alternative 5: Threshold at CV ≥ 0.05 or CV ≥ 0.30 instead of 0.15**  
*Weakness:* CV ≥ 0.05 is too permissive—it would classify near-deterministic genes (e.g., those with Poisson-level fluctuations, CV = 1/√mean ≈ 0.03 for mean = 1000) as "variable," reducing discriminating power against H₀. CV ≥ 0.30 is too stringent—it would miss genes with moderate but biologically significant noise (e.g., bursty low-abundance regulators with CV ≈ 0.2). The 0.15 threshold is grounded in experimental single-cell studies (Elowitz 2002, Taniguchi 2010) showing typical CV for intrinsic noise is ~0.1–0.3; the median of this range provides a robust, pre-registered cutoff that is neither trivially permissive nor unrealistically stringent.

**Alternative 6: Compare wildtype/basal at generation 10 vs. generation 20 (temporal reproducibility)**  
*Weakness:* This tests whether the *system* reaches a stationary distribution, not whether *cells* are identical. If CV at generation 10 and generation 20 are both 0.15, this confirms steady state but does not adjudicate H₁ vs. H₀. The question is about cell-to-cell variation, not temporal stability. The operationalization already assumes generation 10 is sufficient for steady state (stated in auxiliary assumptions); testing this assumption is orthogonal to the hypothesis.

## Concessions

**Threshold arbitrariness:**  
The 0.15 CV cutoff, while empirically motivated, is a convention. Genes with CV = 0.14 would be classified as non-variable, potentially underestimating the prevalence of noise. A sensitivity analysis (re-running the test at CV = 0.10, 0.15, 0.20) would strengthen the claim, but is not included in the operationalization. The binomial test is robust to moderate threshold shifts, but the boundary is inherently somewhat arbitrary.

**
results.brief_agent:
## Thesis
This operationalization is correct because it directly measures **distributional heterogeneity in protein abundance across isogenic replicates**, which is the precise biological phenomenon the question asks about, while using coefficient of variation as a statistically principled, scale-invariant metric that discriminates intrinsic stochastic noise from both measurement artifact and rival biological mechanisms.

---

## Why this operationalization is faithful to the question

**Mapping of constructs:**

1. **"Genetically identical cells"** → `isogenic stochastic replicates (seeds ≥30) of wildtype/basal design`  
   Each seed represents one cell with identical genome and environment; the instrument's seed parameter explicitly varies only intrinsic stochastic trajectories, not genotype or conditions.

2. **"Same gene"** → `named protein species readout`  
   The instrument provides protein copy number per species; selecting the same protein species across seeds means measuring the same gene's expression product.

3. **"Expression level"** → `protein copy number per cell`  
   Protein abundance is the functional readout of gene expression (mRNA is intermediate; protein is what acts). The instrument reports absolute copy number per cell at single-cell resolution.

4. **"Express the same ... at the same level"** → `CV ≤ 0.05 (H0) vs CV > 0.2 (H1)`  
   "Same level" requires low cell-to-cell variation; CV (coefficient of variation = σ/μ) is the standard statistical measure of relative dispersion, scale-invariant across genes with different mean abundances. The threshold 0.05 represents negligible variation (~5% noise, near measurement error); 0.2 represents substantial biological heterogeneity.

**Nothing essential is dropped:**  
- The question is about **population heterogeneity among identical cells**, which is precisely what distributing measurements across seeds captures.  
- Using protein copy number (not mRNA, not flux) reflects the biological reality that protein levels determine phenotype.  

**Nothing is smuggled in:**  
- The claim does not presuppose a *mechanism* (intrinsic noise vs extrinsic noise vs cell-cycle effects) — it only operationalizes *what to measure* (protein CV). The mechanistic hypothesis (intrinsic stochasticity) is tested explicitly via rivals that discriminate mechanisms.

---

## Why it is decisive

**Falsifiability:**  
The decision rule is binary and quantitative:  
- Compute CV for ≥10 genes across ≥30 seeds at steady state.  
- Reject H1 if <50% of genes show CV > 0.2 **or** if median CV ≤ 0.1.  

This creates a clear bright line. A world where cells express genes uniformly (H0) would yield median CV ≤ 0.1 and most genes with CV ≤ 0.05. A world with substantial intrinsic noise (H1) would yield median CV ≈ 0.25 and >50% of genes with CV > 0.2.

**Discriminating power against rivals:**

1. **Rival: Extrinsic noise (global cellular state fluctuations)**  
   - **Distinguishing test:** Measure pairwise Pearson correlation of protein copy numbers for unrelated genes across seeds.  
   - **Discrimination:** Extrinsic noise (shared ribosome/resource fluctuations) couples expression of all genes → high correlations (r > 0.7). Intrinsic noise (independent per-gene transcription/translation bursts) → weak correlations (r < 0.3).  
   - **Why feasible:** The instrument reports `protein_conc` for arbitrary species and `ribosome_conc` globally; computing correlation across seeds is straightforward statistics on accessible readouts.

2. **Rival: Cell-cycle/physiological state variation**  
   - **Distinguishing test:** Normalize protein copy number by global reporters (`dry_mass`, `ribosome_conc`, `cell_mass`) and recompute CV.  
   - **Discrimination:** If variation is due to cell-size or growth-rate heterogeneity, normalization collapses CV to ≤0.1. If intrinsic noise dominates, CV remains >0.15 post-normalization.  
   - **Why feasible:** All normalizing channels (`dry_mass`, `ribosome_conc`, `cell_mass`) are available as instrument readouts; division and CV recalculation require no new measurements.

**Feasibility on this instrument:**  
- The instrument natively supports `seeds` (independent stochastic replicates) and reports protein copy number per species.  
- `wildtype/basal` is the reference design, explicitly noted.  
- 50 seeds × 10 generations × protein readout at steady state is computationally straightforward.  
- Surveying ≥10 genes spanning expression ranges (high/medium/low) is trivial given the whole-cell model tracks ~hundreds of protein species.

---

## Why any other operationalization is weaker

**Alternative 1: Use mRNA copy number instead of protein**  
- **Failure mode (less faithful):** mRNA is an *intermediate*, not the functional output. The question asks whether cells "express the same gene at the same level" — in biology, "expression level" colloquially means the phenotypic consequence (protein), not the transcript. Moreover, mRNA noise is often buffered by translational averaging, so mRNA CV may *overestimate* phenotypic variation.  
- **Failure mode (less decisive):** mRNA noise and protein noise have different timescales and amplitudes; using mRNA could confound transcriptional bursting with post-transcriptional regulation, weakening discrimination of intrinsic stochastic mechanisms.

**Alternative 2: Use mean protein level (not CV) as the test statistic**  
- **Failure mode (less faithful):** The question asks whether cells express "the same" level, which is about *variation*, not central tendency. Comparing means across seeds tests whether *populations* differ, not whether *individual cells* within a population are heterogeneous. Mean is blind to dispersion.  
- **Failure mode (less decisive):** For isogenic seeds in identical conditions, means should trivially match under both H0 and H1 (no systematic bias). The discriminating feature is the *width* of the distribution, not its center. CV directly captures this.

**Alternative 3: Test only a single high-expression constitutive gene (e.g., ribosomal protein)**  
- **Failure mode (less faithful):** The question asks about "the same gene" (singular but generic), implying the phenomenon should hold broadly. Testing one gene risks idiosyncrasy (e.g., highly expressed genes have low CV due to averaging, but rare genes are noisier). A single gene cannot represent "expression" as a general phenomenon.  
- **Failure mode (less decisive):** If only a high-copy constitutive gene is tested, H1 could be rejected spuriously (CV ≈ 0.1 due to high copy number) even though low-copy genes show CV > 0.4. Testing ≥10 genes across expression ranges prevents cherry-picking and ensures the claim is genome-representative.

**Alternative 4: Use variance instead of coefficient of variation**  
- **Failure mode (less faithful):** Variance is scale-dependent. A high-expression gene with 1000 ± 200 copies (CV = 0.2) has variance = 40,000. A low-expression gene with 10 ± 2 copies (CV = 0.2) has variance = 4. Setting a single variance threshold cannot meaningfully compare genes of different abundances. CV normalizes by mean, making it the correct scale-invariant metric for "same level" across genes.

**Alternative 5: Test at generation 1 (early transient) instead of generation 10 (steady state)**  
- **Failure mode (less faithful):** Early generations may include transient dynamics from initialization artifacts, not reflecting the cell's intrinsic stochastic steady-state behavior. The question implicitly assumes cells in a stable condition ("same environment"), which corresponds to equilibrated dynamics.  
- **Failure mode (less feasible):** Generation 1 may not have equilibrated; auxiliary assumption states "transient dynamics equilibrated by generation 10." Testing too early risks confounding initialization noise with intrinsic gene expression noise.

**Alternative 6: Use fewer seeds (e.g., 5–10) to reduce computational cost**  
- **Failure mode (less decisive):** CV estimation requires adequate sample size for statistical precision. With n=5, the standard error of CV is large (≈CV/√(2(n-1)) ≈ 0.35×CV), making it hard to distinguish CV=0.15 from CV=0.25. With n≥30, SE ≈ 0.13×CV, providing stable estimates and strong statistical power to detect differences between H0 (CV ≤ 0.05) and H1 (CV > 0.2).

---

## Concessions

1. **Choice of CV thresholds (0.05 vs 0.2) is somewhat arbitrary.**  
   While CV < 0.05 is a reasonable "negligible variation" threshold (consistent with technical replicate noise), and CV > 0.2 is widely observed in single-cell RNA-seq/protein data as "substantial biological noise," there is no universal bright line. An alternative could set H0: CV ≤ 0.1 and H1: CV > 0.15, which would still discriminate but with a narrower margin. The current thresholds are conservative (wide separation) to minimize ambiguity, but this choice encodes a judgment call about what counts as "same" vs "different."

2. **Protein copy number is a snapshot, not a time-integrated measure.**  
   Expression "level" could be interpreted as time-averaged protein abundance, whereas a single-timepoint readout (generation 10) captures instantaneous copy number. If protein lifetime varies across genes, snapshot CV may not reflect functional steady-state noise. However, the instrument does not provide time-averaged protein output; extending to time-courses would require multi-timepoint sampling, increasing complexity without a clear gain in faithfulness to the question as stated.

3. **Surveying ≥10 genes requires manual selection.**  
   The claim states genes should span high/medium/low expression and constitutive/regulated modes to be "representative," but the instrument does not provide an automatic genome-wide survey. Selection bias could skew results (e.g., picking only highly expressed genes to artificially lower median CV). Mitigating this requires transparent, pre-registered selection criteria (e.g., "top 3 by mean copy number, middle 4 by median, bottom 3"), but the operationalization does not enforce this rigorously.

4. **Assumption that generation 10 is "steady state" may not hold for all genes.**  
   Auxiliary assumption states transients equilibrate by generation 10, but for very slow-turnover proteins or genes with long regulatory delays, equilibration may take longer. If

resp_council.decisive_point: Both operationalizations test the same core phenomenon (protein-level CV across isogenic seeds at steady state), use the same statistic (coefficient of variation), the same design (wildtype/basal, 50 seeds, generation 10), and discriminate the same rivals (extrinsic noise via normalization, deterministic chaos via seed variation). The question does not specify whether the CV threshold boundary should be 0.15 (mine) or 0.2 (theirs), nor whether 20 pre-specified genes (mine) or ≥10 representative genes (theirs) better operationalizes "the same gene." These are methodological refinements, not fundamental differences in construct validity.
resp_council.rationale: Our operationalizations are substantively identical in all elements that matter for construct validity: both measure protein copy number (not mRNA) across stochastic seeds (not temporal variance within a lineage), use CV (not variance) to ensure scale-invariance, normalize by protein_mass or global reporters to discriminate intrinsic from extrinsic noise, and test at steady state (generation 10). The rival-discrimination strategies are equivalent: I use protein[GENE_ID]/protein_mass normalization; they use dry_mass or ribosome_conc normalization—both isolate intrinsic noise. The differences lie in (1) CV threshold (0.15 vs 0.2) and (2) gene-panel specification (20 named genes vs ≥10 spanning expression ranges). Neither threshold is definitively "correct" (both cite single-cell literature; the true biological boundary is continuous, not discrete). Neither gene-panel approach is strictly superior: my 20-gene list is explicit and reproducible but risks omitting edge cases; their ≥10 representative approach is flexible but requires transparent selection criteria to avoid bias (a point they concede). The raw question does not specify these details, so the tie reflects that both operationalizations are equipotent translations of an under-specified question.

resp_agent.decisive_point: Whether the question demands genome-representative sampling (≥10 genes, flexible selection) or a fixed pre-specified gene panel (20 genes, named a priori) to adjudicate "same gene" as a generalizable biological claim versus a hypothesis about specific loci.
resp_agent.rationale: Both operationalizations measure the identical core phenomenon (CV of protein copy number across isogenic seeds at steady state) with nearly identical thresholds (theirs: CV ≥0.15 for ≥50% of genes; mine: CV >0.2 for >50%, median ~0.25), and both employ protein_mass normalization to discriminate intrinsic from extrinsic noise. The methodological divergence is **gene selection strategy**: my approach specifies ≥10 genes "spanning high/medium/low expression" without naming them, risking post-hoc selection bias but allowing flexibility; their approach fixes 20 named genes (rpoB, rpoC, ..., lrp) a priori, eliminating cherry-picking but presupposing those 20 are representative. The raw question "Do genetically identical cells express the same gene at the same level?" is ambiguous on this point—does "gene" (singular, generic) mean "any given gene tested" (permitting flexible sampling) or "a defined gene" (requiring pre-specification)? Neither operationalization is *weaker*; they encode different, equally defensible interpretations of scope. My threshold (CV >0.2) is slightly more conservative than theirs (≥0.15), but both lie within the empirical range from single-cell literature; this difference would rarely reverse a verdict. Both designs are feasible, falsifiable, and discriminate extrinsic noise via the same normalization channel. The tie-breaker missing from the question is: **Must the test be reproducible by a third party using the exact same gene list (favoring pre-specification), or should it be adaptable to the instrument's accessible readouts (favoring sampling criteria)?**

results.outcome_why: Both sides judged the operationalizations equipotent or the question too under-specified to separate them.
results.synthesis: Both counsel independently returned a verdict of TIE, and their reasoning converges on the same substantive conclusion: the two operationalizations are equipotent translations of the research question rather than one being stronger than the other. Both sides agree that the operationalizations measure the identical core phenomenon—the coefficient of variation of protein copy number across isogenic seeds at steady state—using the same design, the same scale-invariant statistic, and the same protein_mass normalization to discriminate intrinsic from extrinsic noise. The only divergences either side could identify were methodological refinements rather than differences in construct validity: a CV threshold of 0.15 versus 0.2 (both within the empirical range and unlikely to reverse a verdict), and a gene-selection strategy of 20 pre-specified named genes versus ≥10 representative genes chosen flexibly. Crucially, both counsel attribute this inability to separate the operationalizations to under-specification in the raw question itself, which does not fix a threshold boundary nor clarify whether "the same gene" means a defined a priori panel (favoring reproducibility) or any representative gene (favoring flexible sampling). The question therefore could not distinguish the two operationalizations because each encodes an equally defensible interpretation of ambiguities the question leaves open.

results.question: What makes one gene's expression noisier than another's?

hyp_council.question: What makes one gene's expression noisier than another's?
hyp_council.claim: Gene expression noise (CV of protein copy-number across isogenic replicates) is primarily determined by the gene's mean expression level, with low-expression genes exhibiting higher relative noise due to the stochastic birth-death regime, following an inverse square-root scaling law (CV ∝ 1/√mean).
hyp_council.h1: Expression noise (coefficient of variation of protein copy-number) scales inversely with the square root of mean expression across genes: CV ∝ 1/√mean, with low-expression genes showing CV > 0.3 and high-expression genes showing CV < 0.1.
hyp_council.h0: Expression noise (CV) is independent of mean expression level; the coefficient of variation is uniform across genes regardless of their mean copy-number.

operational_defs.observable: protein species copy-number across isogenic replicates (seeds) at steady-state
operational_defs.measure: coefficient of variation (CV = σ/μ) computed from ≥20 seeds per gene
arithmetic mean across ≥20 seeds at steady-state (final generation)
mean copy-number from steady-state samples
mean copy-number from steady-state samples

hyp_council.predicted_effect: Power-law relationship: log(CV) vs log(mean) with slope ≈ -0.5 (inverse square-root scaling), R² ≥ 0.7 across ≥10 genes spanning 2+ orders of magnitude in mean expression. Low-expression genes (mean <50 copies): CV ≥ 0.3. High-expression genes (mean >500 copies): CV ≤ 0.1.

falsifier.channel: protein species copy-number for ≥10 genes (acrB, lacZ, rpoB, tufA, ompF, crp, fis, dnaK, lon, clpB)
falsifier.decision_rule: OLS regression of log(CV) on log(mean) across ≥10 genes; reject H0 if slope 95% CI excludes 0 and includes -0.5, AND R² ≥ 0.7. Reject H1 if slope 95% CI excludes -0.5 OR R² < 0.5.
falsifier.refuting_result: Slope 95% CI excludes -0.5, OR R² < 0.5, OR no significant negative correlation (slope CI includes 0).

rivals.claim: Expression noise is determined primarily by transcriptional burst size (translation per mRNA), not mean expression level. Genes with high burst parameters (strong RBS, stable mRNA) show elevated noise even at high mean expression.
rivals.distinguishing_result: Introducing a new_gene with artificially high RBS strength (high translation rate) but moderate mean expression (~200 copies) would show CV > 0.25, deviating above the inverse square-root prediction. Conversely, a gene_knockout of an RNase (e.g., rne) increasing mRNA half-life would shift noise upward across all expression levels, breaking the scaling law.

rivals.claim: Expression noise reflects extrinsic physiological fluctuations (growth rate, ribosome concentration variability) rather than intrinsic gene parameters. All genes share correlated noise driven by cell-state variation.
rivals.distinguishing_result: Clamping ppgpp_conc (e.g., ppgpp_conc at 1.5× basal) to reduce growth-rate fluctuations would uniformly compress CV across all genes by ≥30%, preserving rank order but violating the predicted absolute CV magnitudes. Alternatively, computing pairwise protein correlations across seeds would show Pearson r > 0.6 for most gene pairs, indicating dominant extrinsic noise.

auxiliary_assumptions: Steady-state is achieved by generation 8 under basal condition; transient dynamics do not dominate the CV measurement.
The simulator's stochastic engine faithfully represents intrinsic gene-expression noise (partitioning at division, stochastic transcription/translation events).
Protein degradation rates and mRNA half-lives are modeled with sufficient fidelity to reproduce the birth-death noise regime.

The selected gene panel spans a sufficient dynamic range (≥2 orders of magnitude in mean expression) and is not confounded by operonic co-regulation or post-translational modifications outside the model scope.
Extrinsic noise sources (cell-cycle stage variation, spatial heterogeneity) are either negligible or captured by the seed-to-seed variance in the simulator.
The sample size (30 seeds) provides adequate statistical power to estimate CV with <10% relative error for genes with mean >20 copies.
residual_ambiguities: [rival_not_excluded] The hypothesis tests a genome-wide perturbation (rne knockout) that elevates mRNA stability for all genes simultaneously, and measures an aggregate channel (dry_mass) that integrates contributions from all proteins. This design cannot identify which individual genes' expression becomes noisier or explain why one gene differs from another in noise. The user's question asks for gene-to-gene comparative determinants ('What makes one gene's expression noisier than another's?'), but the hypothesis addresses only whether a global shift in burst size increases aggregate cell-to-cell variation. The hypothesis does not provide a mechanism or test to distinguish gene A from gene B, nor does it predict which genes within the genome will exhibit elevated noise and which will not. To answer the user's question, the design must measure per-gene protein copy-number variation (or a valid per-gene proxy) and compare noise across multiple genes with varying intrinsic properties (e.g., different RBS strengths, mRNA stabilities, or promoter architectures), or use a perturbation that affects only a subset of genes, enabling gene-level discrimination.

hyp_agent.question: What makes one gene's expression noisier than another's?
hyp_agent.claim: Gene expression noise (protein-level coefficient of variation across isogenic replicates) is primarily determined by transcription burst size: genes with larger transcriptional bursts (higher mRNA-to-protein translation efficiency or longer mRNA half-life) exhibit higher protein copy-number noise, following the prediction CV_protein ∝ 1/√(burst_size × mean_protein).
hyp_agent.h1: Protein copy-number noise (CV across seeds) differs systematically between genes and scales inversely with the square root of mean expression level, with residual variance explained by transcription burst parameters (mRNA stability and translation rate). Genes with high burst size (long-lived mRNA or high translation rate) show elevated noise at matched mean abundance.
hyp_agent.h0: Protein copy-number noise is uniform across genes at matched mean expression level; intrinsic noise does not depend on gene-specific transcription or translation parameters.

operational_defs.measure: coefficient of variation (std/mean) of protein copy number across seeds (isogenic replicates) in steady-state exponential growth
mean copy number across seeds
ratio of protein CV to mRNA CV, or protein-per-mRNA ratio (translation efficiency proxy)
mean mRNA copy number at matched transcription rate (higher mRNA at fixed transcription implies longer half-life)

hyp_agent.predicted_effect: Across a panel of ≥8 genes spanning ≥2 orders of magnitude in mean protein abundance: (1) log(CV_protein) vs log(mean_protein) yields a negative slope between -0.4 and -0.6 (approaching the -0.5 Poisson limit), R²≥0.6. (2) At matched mean protein (within 2-fold), genes in the top tertile of mRNA-to-protein ratio show ≥1.5-fold higher CV than genes in the bottom tertile.

falsifier.channel: protein[panel: acrB, lacZ, ompF, rpoD, ftsZ, dnaA, lacI, crp]
falsifier.decision_rule: OLS regression of log(CV_protein) on log(mean_protein) across ≥8 genes (each gene mean and CV computed over ≥20 seeds). Reject H0 if slope 95% CI excludes 0, slope is between -0.4 and -0.6, and R²≥0.6. Additionally, partition genes into tertiles by mRNA/protein ratio; reject H0 if top tertile mean CV ≥ 1.5× bottom tertile mean CV (Welch t, |t|≥2) among genes matched within 2-fold mean protein.
falsifier.refuting_result: Slope 95% CI includes 0, or slope is outside [-0.6, -0.4], or R²<0.6, or the top-tertile CV is <1.5× bottom-tertile CV or |t|<2.

rivals.claim: Expression noise is determined solely by mean abundance (Poisson shot noise), with no additional gene-specific burst contribution.
rivals.distinguishing_result: log(CV) vs log(mean) slope would be exactly -0.5 and R²≥0.95 with no residual variance; partitioning by mRNA/protein ratio would show <1.2-fold difference in CV between tertiles (|t|<1).

rivals.claim: Expression noise is dominated by extrinsic factors (cell-to-cell variation in growth rate, ribosome concentration, or ppGpp) rather than gene-intrinsic burst parameters.
rivals.distinguishing_result: Clamping ppGpp (ppgpp_conc at 1.0× basal) or ribosome capacity (rrna_operon_knockout to reduce global translation noise) would reduce CV uniformly across all genes by ≥30%, collapsing the mRNA/protein-ratio effect to <1.2-fold.

rivals.claim: Noise differences arise from post-translational protein degradation rate variation, not transcriptional bursting.
rivals.distinguishing_result: Genes with high protein turnover (inferred from low protein/mRNA ratio at matched mRNA) would show higher CV; the mRNA/protein ratio effect would reverse sign (low ratio → high CV). Additionally, knocking out lon or clpP proteases would uniformly reduce CV across high-turnover genes by ≥40%.

auxiliary_assumptions: The simulator faithfully represents gene-specific mRNA synthesis rates, mRNA degradation rates, translation rates, and protein degradation rates for the panel of genes tested.
Steady-state exponential growth is achieved by generation 8, so that CV reflects intrinsic noise rather than transient adaptation.
The chosen gene panel (acrB, lacZ, ompF, rpoD, ftsZ, dnaA, lacI, crp) spans sufficient dynamic range in mean expression and burst parameters to reveal the predicted scaling.
Measurement of protein and mRNA copy numbers is accurate and not confounded by detection limits or saturation.
The basal condition does not impose stringent-response or other stress that would dominate intrinsic gene-level noise sources.
The simulator's stochastic engine (SSA or tau-leaping) correctly samples intrinsic biochemical noise at the single-cell level.

results.brief_council:
## Thesis

This operationalization is correct because it directly measures the variance structure of single-gene protein abundance across biological replicates at multiple mean expression levels, thereby isolating the statistical signature that distinguishes gene-intrinsic stochastic parameters from alternative noise sources.

---

## Why this operationalization is faithful to the question

The raw question asks *what makes one gene's expression noisier than another's* — a comparative, gene-level inquiry into determinants of heterogeneity.

**Construct mappings:**

- **"gene's expression"** → `protein species copy-number` for individual genes (acrB, lacZ, rpoB, etc.). The instrument provides per-species protein measurement; this is the direct molecular readout of expression outcome.

- **"noisier"** → `coefficient of variation (CV = σ/μ)` computed across `seeds` (isogenic replicates). Seeds vary only intrinsic stochastic events (partitioning, birth-death), making CV the standard measure of relative variability. Absolute variance would confound magnitude with noise; CV normalizes by mean, isolating *relative* fluctuation.

- **"one gene … than another's"** → Cross-gene comparison via regression across ≥10 genes. The hypothesis predicts a systematic relationship (CV ∝ 1/√mean), providing a quantitative law that distinguishes genes by their mean expression level.

- **"what makes"** (causal determinant) → The inverse square-root scaling law attributes noise differences to **mean expression level** as the governing variable. Low-copy genes (mean <50) operate in the regime where stochastic birth-death dominates (Poisson-like, CV ≈ 1/√N); high-copy genes (>500) approach deterministic averages (CV → 0). This is a mechanistic hypothesis grounded in stochastic chemical kinetics.

**Nothing essential is dropped:** The question asks for comparative noise determinants; the operationalization tests whether a single gene-intrinsic parameter (mean level) predicts noise magnitude. **Nothing smuggled in:** The power-law claim is falsifiable; the null (CV independent of mean) is a legitimate rival. The gene panel spans two orders of magnitude, ensuring the dynamic range necessary to detect or refute scaling.

---

## Why it is decisive

**Falsifiability:**

- **Precise quantitative prediction:** slope ≈ −0.5 in log-log space, R² ≥ 0.7. The null predicts slope ≈ 0 (no relationship). These are mutually exclusive.
- **Decision rule is mechanical:** OLS regression with 95% CI. If CI excludes −0.5 OR R² < 0.5, H1 is rejected. If CI excludes 0, H0 is rejected. Both outcomes are possible, so the test is genuinely falsifiable.
- **Threshold criteria are realistic:** R² ≥ 0.7 is appropriate for biological data with intrinsic variance; lower R² would indicate other factors dominate. The slope CI must *include* −0.5 but *exclude* 0, cleanly discriminating the hypotheses.

**Discriminating power against named rivals:**

1. **Burst-size rival:** Claims transcriptional burst parameters (RBS strength, mRNA stability) drive noise independently of mean. The `new_gene` design with artificially high RBS at moderate mean (~200 copies) tests this: if the rival is correct, CV > 0.25 (above the −0.5 slope prediction for mean=200, which is ~0.07). If H1 is correct, the gene falls on the power-law curve. The *direction* of deviation discriminates.

2. **Extrinsic-noise rival:** Claims cell-state fluctuations (growth rate, ribosome variability) create correlated noise across all genes. The `ppgpp_conc` clamp design tests this: clamping ppGpp reduces growth-rate variance. If extrinsic noise dominates, CV compresses uniformly across genes (≥30% reduction), shifting the intercept but preserving rank order. If H1 is correct, the *slope* remains −0.5 and absolute CV values are preserved (since mean-dependent intrinsic noise is unaffected by growth clamping). Additionally, if extrinsic noise dominates, pairwise protein correlations across seeds should be high (r > 0.6); if intrinsic noise dominates, correlations should be low. The rival predicts uniform compression + high correlation; H1 predicts slope preservation + low correlation.

**Feasibility:**

- The instrument measures `protein species copy-number` for specified genes. The 10-gene panel (acrB, lacZ, rpoB, tufA, ompF, crp, fis, dnaK, lon, clpB) spans ribosomal proteins (high expression), metabolic enzymes (moderate), and stress-response regulators (low to moderate), ensuring 2+ orders of magnitude range.
- 30 seeds at 8 generations (steady-state per auxiliary assumption) provides statistical power: for a Poisson-like process with mean ≥20, 30 replicates yield CV estimation error <10%, sufficient to resolve the predicted CV range (0.1–0.3+).
- OLS regression with 10 data points (genes) provides adequate degrees of freedom for slope + intercept estimation and CI construction.

---

## Why any other operationalization is weaker

**Alternative 1: Measure mRNA copy-number CV instead of protein.**

*Weakness:* mRNA noise is upstream and often amplified by translational bursting; it does not directly answer "gene expression" (the functional readout is protein). Moreover, the inverse square-root law is derived for birth-death at the protein level (dilution by growth + degradation). mRNA has different kinetics (faster turnover, no dilution in some models). The scaling exponent for mRNA may differ (often closer to −1 in purely transcriptional models). This operationalization is **less faithful** because "expression" typically refers to protein abundance in functional genomics, and **less decisive** because the predicted exponent is ambiguous.

**Alternative 2: Use Fano factor (variance/mean) instead of CV.**

*Weakness:* Fano factor is unnormalized and increases with mean for super-Poissonian processes. It does not isolate *relative* noise, which is what "noisier" connotes in comparative contexts (a low-copy gene with high absolute variance may be less noisy *relative to its mean* than a high-copy gene). The question asks what makes one gene noisier *than another*, implying a normalized comparison. Fano factor confounds magnitude with variability. This is **less faithful** to the construct "noisier" (which implies relative fluctuation) and **less decisive** because the rival null (Fano ∝ mean⁰, i.e., Poisson) vs. H1 (Fano ∝ mean⁰·⁵, super-Poisson) is harder to distinguish empirically (both predict increasing Fano with mean; only the exponent differs, requiring tighter power-law fitting).

**Alternative 3: Measure noise for a single gene across different conditions (e.g., varying ppGpp or nutrient) instead of across genes at steady-state.**

*Weakness:* This tests how *one gene's* noise changes with physiological state, not *what distinguishes one gene from another*. The question is explicitly comparative across genes, not across conditions for one gene. Varying condition introduces extrinsic confounders (growth rate, global stress responses) that obscure gene-intrinsic parameters. This is **less faithful** (does not compare genes) and **less decisive** (cannot isolate mean expression as the discriminator; condition-dependent effects conflate multiple variables).

**Alternative 4: Use a broader gene panel (50–100 genes) but measure only wildtype, no perturbations.**

*Strength:* Greater statistical power for the power-law fit. *Weakness:* Without perturbations (new_gene, ppgpp_conc), the design cannot discriminate the named rivals. The wildtype-only design is **less decisive**: if burst size or extrinsic noise *also* scales with mean (plausible, since highly expressed genes may have different RBS or be more buffered), the wildtype correlation could reflect either mechanism. The perturbations are critical to *break* the correlation under alternative hypotheses. Larger N without rival-testing is **less decisive** even if more precise.

**Alternative 5: Measure aggregate channel (e.g., dry_mass or protein_mass CV) across seeds.**

*Weakness:* Aggregate mass integrates all proteins, obscuring gene-level differences. The question asks what makes *one gene* noisier than *another*; dry_mass cannot distinguish per-gene contributions. Two genes with identical mass contributions but different relative noise would be invisible. This is **fatally less faithful** (does not measure gene-level variation) and **non-decisive** (cannot compare genes, the core of the question).

**Alternative 6: Define "noisier" as temporal fluctuation (CV across timepoints within one seed) instead of CV across seeds.**

*Weakness:* Temporal noise within a lineage conflates cell-cycle dynamics, deterministic oscillations, and transient responses with intrinsic stochasticity. The question's "noisier" in gene-expression contexts canonically refers to cell-to-cell variability in isogenic populations (the classic phenotypic noise literature). Temporal CV is **less faithful** to standard constructs and **less decisive** because steady-state temporal fluctuations may be tiny (if the gene is at equilibrium, temporal CV → 0), masking the biological variation across a population.

---

## Concessions

1. **Gene-panel bias:** The selected 10 genes (acrB, lacZ, rpoB, tufA, ompF, crp, fis, dnaK, lon, clpB) are not random; they are likely canonical, well-characterized genes. If the simulator's parameterization is tuned to reproduce known behavior for these specific genes, the power-law fit may be artificially good. A randomly selected panel might show weaker R² if gene-specific regulatory quirks (e.g., autoregulation, post-translational control) introduce noise orthogonal to mean expression. The operationalization assumes these 10 genes are representative, which is debatable.

2. **Steady-state assumption:** The claim that generation 8 represents steady-state is an auxiliary assumption, not tested. If some genes equilibrate slower (e.g., long-lived proteins like Lon protease), their CV at generation 8 may underestimate true steady-state noise. The operationalization does not include a time-series check to confirm equilibrium; this is a hidden dependency.

3. **Translation of "noisier":** CV is one of several noise metrics (Fano factor, noise strength η² = σ²/μ², etc.). While CV is standard, the choice is not uniquely determined by the raw question. results.brief_agent:
## Thesis
This operationalization is correct because it decomposes "noisier" into the coefficient of variation of protein copy number across isogenic replicates and directly tests the mechanistic hypothesis that residual variance beyond Poisson shot noise arises from gene-specific transcriptional burst parameters—mRNA stability and translation rate—measured as the mRNA-to-protein ratio, using a multi-gene panel to reveal systematic scaling relationships that pure shot-noise or purely extrinsic models cannot produce.

## Why this operationalization is faithful to the question

**"One gene's expression"** → `protein[GENE_X]` measured as copy number per cell in steady-state exponential growth (generation 8). Expression is operationalized at the protein level because proteins execute cellular functions; mRNA is measured as an auxiliary observable to infer burst parameters.

**"noisier than another's"** → comparative coefficient of variation (std/mean) across seeds. "Noisier" means larger relative fluctuations in an isogenic population; CV is the standard dimensionless measure of relative variability, enabling comparison across genes with different mean abundances. The seeds parameter generates isogenic replicates varying only in intrinsic biochemical noise, isolating gene-intrinsic stochasticity from environmental heterogeneity.

**"What makes"** → mechanistic claim linking noise magnitude to burst size. The question demands a causal explanation, not just a description. Burst size is operationalized as the mRNA-to-protein ratio (high ratio = many proteins per mRNA = large burst) or mean mRNA copy number at matched transcription rate (high mRNA = long half-life = large burst). These are measurable proxies for the latent parameters (translation rate per mRNA, mRNA degradation rate) that control burst size in stochastic gene expression theory.

**Nothing essential is dropped:** The raw question asks what distinguishes genes' noise levels. The operationalization tests whether intrinsic biochemical parameters (burst size) explain systematic differences, using a scaling relationship (CV vs. mean) to separate intrinsic from trivial Poisson effects, and a tertile comparison to isolate the burst-size effect at matched mean abundance.

**Nothing smuggled in:** The claim restricts to steady-state exponential growth to avoid transient dynamics, uses basal condition to avoid stress responses that might mask intrinsic noise sources, and requires ≥8 genes spanning ≥2 orders of magnitude to ensure the scaling relationship is robust, not an artifact of a narrow dynamic range.

## Why it is decisive

**Falsifiability:** The decision rule specifies four independent criteria, each with quantitative thresholds: (1) slope 95% CI must exclude 0 (noise systematically varies with mean), (2) slope must lie in [-0.6, -0.4] (consistent with the √mean Poisson limit plus burst contributions, not arbitrary scaling), (3) R² ≥ 0.6 (burst parameters explain substantial variance beyond Poisson), (4) top-tertile CV ≥ 1.5× bottom-tertile CV with |t| ≥ 2 (burst-size effect is large and statistically robust). Each criterion can independently fail, making the operationalization sharply falsifiable. The refuting result is the logical negation: any one criterion failure rejects H₁.

**Discriminating power against named rivals:**

- **Rival 1 (pure Poisson):** Predicts slope exactly -0.5, R² ≥ 0.95, <1.2-fold CV difference between burst-size tertiles. Our operationalization expects slope in [-0.6, -0.4] (allowing deviation from -0.5), R² ≥ 0.6 but <0.95 (residual variance from burst heterogeneity), and ≥1.5-fold tertile difference. If the data show -0.5 slope and R² near 1, our claim is disconfirmed and pure Poisson is supported.

- **Rival 2 (extrinsic dominance):** If extrinsic factors (growth rate, ribosome fluctuations, ppGpp) dominate, clamping ppGpp or deleting rRNA operons should uniformly reduce all genes' CV by ≥30%, collapsing the mRNA/protein-ratio effect to <1.2-fold. The designs include `ppgpp_conc` (clamp ppGpp) and `rrna_operon_knockout` (reduce global translation noise) as mechanistic tests. If these perturbations eliminate the tertile effect, extrinsic noise is dominant and our intrinsic-burst claim fails.

- **Rival 3 (protein degradation):** Predicts genes with high protein turnover (low protein/mRNA ratio) show high CV. Our operationalization predicts the opposite: high protein/mRNA ratio (large burst) → high CV. The sign of the mRNA/protein-ratio effect distinguishes the two. Additionally, if knocking out `lon` or `clpP` proteases reduces CV in high-turnover genes by ≥40%, degradation dominates; this is testable via `gene_knockout` perturbation (though not explicitly in candidate designs, it's mechanistically distinguishable).

**Feasibility on this instrument:** The dial labels confirm `protein[GENE_X]` and `mrna[GENE_X]` are directly measurable. The gene panel (acrB, lacZ, ompF, rpoD, ftsZ, dnaA, lacI, crp) uses familiar E. coli genes likely implemented in the simulator. The `wildtype` perturbation with 30 seeds and 8 generations is a standard baseline design. The `disconfirm(target, reference, channel)` call with `welch_t ≥ 2` provides the statistical test. The perturbations (`ppgpp_conc`, `rrna_operon_knockout`) are explicitly listed dial labels, confirming feasibility.

## Why any other operationalization is weaker

**Alternative 1: Measure mRNA noise instead of protein noise.**  
mRNA fluctuations are upstream of protein output; the biological question "gene expression noise" typically concerns the functional output (protein). mRNA CV conflates transcriptional bursting with mRNA lifetime effects differently than protein CV does (protein integrates mRNA fluctuations over translation). Measuring only mRNA cannot distinguish whether high protein noise arises from transcriptional bursting or translational amplification. **Failure mode:** Misses the biologically relevant layer where noise impacts cellular function; cannot test burst-size predictions that depend on mRNA-to-protein coupling.

**Alternative 2: Use Fano factor (variance/mean) instead of CV.**  
Fano factor is unnormalized and confounds absolute abundance with noise. Comparing Fano factors across genes with different means is uninformative without additional normalization. The Poisson limit for Fano is 1 (variance = mean), but this does not scale with mean; a gene with mean 10 and Fano 1 has CV 0.32, while mean 1000 and Fano 1 has CV 0.032. **Failure mode:** Cannot distinguish systematic noise differences from trivial abundance differences; the scaling law (CV ∝ 1/√mean) is obscured.

**Alternative 3: Compare noise across genes at a single time point (e.g., generation 1) rather than steady state.**  
Early-generation measurements conflate transient adaptation noise (initial-condition sensitivity, early stochastic fate decisions) with intrinsic steady-state noise. The assumption of exponential growth steady state by generation 8 ensures that measured CV reflects ongoing stochastic biochemistry, not transient dynamics. **Failure mode:** Noise at generation 1 may be dominated by initial-condition artifacts or early cell-cycle asynchrony, not the gene-intrinsic parameters the question targets.

**Alternative 4: Use a narrower gene panel (e.g., 3–4 genes) spanning <1 order of magnitude in mean abundance.**  
The scaling relationship (log CV vs. log mean) requires sufficient dynamic range to fit a slope with precision. With <2 orders of magnitude, the slope estimate has wide confidence intervals, making it hard to distinguish -0.5 (Poisson) from -0.4 or -0.6. Fewer genes reduce statistical power for the tertile comparison. **Failure mode:** Slope 95% CI likely includes 0 or spans a wide range (e.g., [-0.8, -0.2]), failing the decisiveness criterion; the R² threshold (≥0.6) becomes arbitrary if variance estimates are noisy.

**Alternative 5: Partition genes by mean mRNA copy number instead of mRNA-to-protein ratio.**  
Mean mRNA conflates transcription rate with mRNA stability; high mRNA could result from high transcription (many small bursts) or long half-life (few large bursts). The mRNA-to-protein ratio isolates the burst size (proteins produced per mRNA molecule) by normalizing out transcription frequency. **Failure mode:** Partitioning by mRNA alone cannot distinguish burst-size variation from transcription-rate variation, weakening the mechanistic interpretation and reducing discriminatory power against the pure-Poisson rival.

**Alternative 6: Test only the scaling law (slope in [-0.6, -0.4], R² ≥ 0.6) without the tertile comparison.**  
The scaling law alone shows that noise varies systematically with mean, but residual variance could arise from measurement error, extrinsic noise, or other uncontrolled factors, not necessarily burst size. The tertile comparison directly tests whether the *mechanistic* predictor (mRNA/protein ratio) explains residual variance at matched mean abundance. **Failure mode:** Without the tertile test, the operationalization is weaker against Rival 2 (extrinsic noise could produce residual variance and a slope deviation from -0.5 without invoking burst size).

**Alternative 7: Rely on `disconfirm()` rather than explicit regression analysis.**  
The `disconfirm(target, reference, channel)` call tests whether a single target differs from reference, returning `welch_t` and effect size. For a scaling relationship across ≥8 genes, `disconfirm()` applied gene-by-gene does not directly test the slope or R². Regression analysis (OLS log-log fit) is the correct statistical framework for a scaling law. **Failure mode:** Pairwise comparisons lose information about the functional form (slope) and goodness-of-fit (R²), making it impossible to distinguish -0.5 from -0.4 or assess how much variance burst size explains.

## Concessions

**Debatable choice 1: The threshold slope range [-0.6, -0.4]** is motivated by theory (Poisson limit -0.5 plus finite burst corrections), but the exact bounds are somewhat arbitrary. A gene with very small burst size might approach -0.5 closely, while very large bursts might push the slope toward -0.6 or beyond. If real data fall at -0.35 or -0.65 with high R², our operationalization rejects H₁ even

resp_council.decisive_point: The opposing operationalization provides a mechanistic decomposition of the inverse square-root scaling by directly testing whether residual variance (beyond the Poisson mean-dependence) is explained by transcriptional burst parameters (mRNA/protein ratio), whereas my operationalization stops at demonstrating the scaling law without isolating the causal mechanism that distinguishes genes at matched mean abundance.
resp_council.rationale: Both operationalizations correctly measure protein copy-number CV across seeds and predict the same inverse square-root scaling law (slope ≈ -0.5, R² ≥ 0.6-0.7) across a comparable gene panel. The critical difference lies in what happens *after* establishing the scaling law. My operationalization treats mean expression level as the primary determinant and relies on perturbations (new_gene with high RBS, ppgpp_conc clamp) to test rivals, but these perturbations change mean expression or global physiology—they do not cleanly isolate burst-size effects at matched mean. The opposing design adds a **within-mean-matched tertile comparison** using the mRNA/protein ratio as a direct observable proxy for burst size, testing whether genes with similar mean abundance but different burst parameters show systematically different CV (≥1.5-fold difference, |t|≥2). This operationalization answers "what makes one gene noisier than another's" more faithfully: it explains why genes *at the same mean* differ in noise, identifying burst size as the gene-intrinsic parameter, whereas my operationalization conflates mean and burst (since both change together in natural genes). The tertile test is feasible (requires measuring both protein[X] and mrna[X] for the same gene panel under wildtype/basal), falsifiable (could show <1.2-fold difference), and decisively discriminates the pure-Poisson rival (which predicts no tertile effect) from the burst-size mechanism. My operationalization is not wrong, but it is mechanistically incomplete: demonstrating CV ∝ 1/√mean is necessary but insufficient to explain what distinguishes genes, because mean itself may be a confound of burst size and transcription rate.

resp_agent.decisive_point: The raw question does not specify whether "what makes one gene noisier" asks for the proximal statistical descriptor (mean expression level as the governing variable in the scaling law) or the mechanistic biochemical cause (burst-size parameters like mRNA stability and translation rate that produce residual variance beyond the mean-driven baseline). Both operationalizations measure the same observable (protein CV across seeds) and test the same scaling relationship (CV ∝ 1/√mean), but interpret "what makes" differently: mine attributes residual variance *above* the Poisson baseline to burst parameters, theirs attributes the entire scaling to mean-level stochasticity.
resp_agent.rationale: Both operationalizations converge on the same core design: measure protein CV across ≥8–10 genes spanning ≥2 orders of magnitude, test log(CV) vs log(mean) with slope in [-0.6, -0.4] (mine) or ≈-0.5 (theirs), R² ≥0.6 (mine) or ≥0.7 (theirs). The statistical framework, gene panel, and falsifier are nearly identical. The key divergence is interpretive: my operationalization adds a tertile comparison by mRNA/protein ratio to isolate *mechanistic* burst-size effects at matched mean, testing whether genes differ in noise *beyond* what mean alone predicts. Theirs treats mean as the sole predictor and tests whether the data conform to the canonical inverse square-root law, with perturbations (high-RBS gene, ppGpp clamp) checking whether burst or extrinsic factors *break* the scaling. My approach asks "does burst size explain residual variance after accounting for mean?" Theirs asks "does mean alone explain the variance, or do other factors dominate?" These are complementary, not contradictory. The raw question "what makes one gene noisier" is ambiguous between a statistical answer (mean level governs the scaling law) and a mechanistic answer (burst parameters cause deviations from that law). Neither operationalization is stronger; the question itself does not specify which layer of causation is sought.

results.outcome_why: The Socratic Council conceded that the Cellarium agent's operationalization is stronger; the agent did not concede.
results.synthesis: Both counsel agree that the two operationalizations share nearly identical core designs—measuring protein copy-number CV across a comparable multi-gene panel and testing the same inverse square-root scaling law (slope ≈ -0.5, comparable R² thresholds)—so they do not differ on the primary observable or the scaling relationship. The decisive contrast turns instead on the added within-mean-matched tertile comparison using the mRNA/protein ratio as a burst-size proxy, which isolates why genes at the same mean abundance differ in noise. The Council yields on the grounds that this tertile test supplies a mechanistic decomposition its own design lacks, treating the isolation of burst size at matched mean as a more faithful answer to "what makes one gene noisier than another's." The Cellarium agent, however, declares a tie, arguing that the raw question is genuinely ambiguous between a proximal statistical descriptor (mean level governs the scaling) and a mechanistic biochemical cause (burst parameters produce residual variance), so the two designs answer complementary rather than competing questions. Taken together, the verdicts imply that the burst-decomposition operationalization is stronger only under a mechanistic reading of the question, and that the residual disagreement reflects an unresolved ambiguity in the question's intended layer of causation rather than a clear superiority on shared criteria.

hyp_council.question: Why do some bacteria survive antibiotics without becoming resistant?
hyp_council.claim: Antibiotic-tolerant persisters arise from low-growth or non-growing subpopulations that stochastically enter a physiological state characterized by low ribosome concentration and elevated ppGpp, which confers transient survival without genetic resistance.
hyp_council.h1: In an isogenic population under basal growth, a fraction of cells stochastically enter a low-growth persister state (growth_rate < 0.1 × population mean) characterized by elevated ppGpp_conc (≥2× population mean) and reduced ribosome_conc (≤0.5× population mean), and this fraction increases under amino-acid starvation.
hyp_council.h0: All cells in an isogenic population maintain growth_rate within stochastic noise of the mean; ppGpp_conc and ribosome_conc distributions are unimodal and tightly centered, with no subpopulation below 0.1× mean growth_rate.

operational_defs.measure: fraction of cells with growth_rate < 0.1 × population mean, measured per seed at end of simulation
mean ppGpp concentration (uM) in low-growth subpopulation vs. population mean
mean ribosome concentration (uM) in low-growth subpopulation vs. population mean
removal of an amino acid mid-run via timeline perturbation

hyp_council.predicted_effect: Under basal growth (wildtype/basal), 5–15% of cells will exhibit growth_rate < 0.1× mean; this low-growth fraction will increase to 25–40% following amino-acid removal (timeline with AA shift at generation 5). The low-growth subpopulation will show ppGpp_conc ≥2× population mean and ribosome_conc ≤0.5× population mean.

falsifier.decision_rule: Classify each cell at final timepoint: persister if growth_rate < 0.1× population mean. Compute fraction across ≥20 seeds. Reject H0 if fraction ≥5% AND the distribution is significantly bimodal (Hartigan dip test p<0.05). Additionally, Welch t-test on ppGpp_conc (low-growth vs. rest); reject if t≥2 and fold-change ≥2.
falsifier.refuting_result: Fraction of low-growth cells <5%, OR growth_rate distribution is unimodal (dip test p≥0.05), OR ppGpp_conc in low-growth cells is not significantly elevated (t<2 or fold-change <2).

rivals.claim: Low growth_rate is an artifact of cell-cycle phase (S/G periods) rather than a distinct persister state; all cells cycle through low-growth phases deterministically.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If this rival is true, the fraction of low-growth cells will be constant (~same as cell-cycle S-phase fraction) and independent of amino-acid starvation. The timeline/AA-removal design will show no increase in low-growth fraction compared to wildtype/basal.

rivals.claim: Elevated ppGpp is a passive consequence of slow growth (correlation) rather than a causal driver of persistence; ppGpp does not regulate entry into the persister state.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If this rival is true, clamping ppGpp_conc to basal levels (ppgpp_conc/basal with multiplier=1.0) will NOT reduce the low-growth fraction under amino-acid starvation, whereas H1 predicts the clamped design will show significantly fewer persisters (fraction <10% vs. 25–40% in unclamped timeline).

rivals.claim: Persisters arise from rare mutants or epigenetic inheritance across generations, not stochastic entry within a single generation.
rivals.distinguishing_result: If this rival is true, the low-growth fraction will increase with generation number (accumulation over lineages) and show strong seed-to-seed variation (some seeds with 0%, others with high fractions). H1 predicts stable fraction across generations and moderate inter-seed CV (<50%).

auxiliary_assumptions: The simulator faithfully represents stochastic gene expression noise and partitioning noise at cell division, sufficient to generate phenotypic heterogeneity in growth_rate within an isogenic population.
The whole-cell model computes single-cell growth_rate, ppGpp_conc, and ribosome_conc at sufficient temporal resolution to detect transient low-growth states within a generation.

The simulator returns per-cell data (growth_rate, ppGpp_conc, ribosome_conc for each cell at each timepoint or at least at the final timepoint), enabling classification of cells by growth_rate and conditional aggregation of ppGpp/ribosome values within the low-growth subpopulation. If only population-level summaries are available, the conditional statistics required by the falsifier and rival #2 are infeasible.

Antibiotic tolerance in vivo correlates with low metabolic activity / low growth rate; the simulator does not explicitly model antibiotic action or survival under antibiotic exposure, so we use growth_rate as a proxy for susceptibility. This hypothesis tests a necessary but not sufficient condition for explaining antibiotic survival: it establishes whether a low-growth subpopulation with elevated ppGpp exists and is modulated by starvation, but does NOT test whether such cells actually survive antibiotic challenge. Validating the causal link to antibiotic survival is outside the simulator's scope.

The timeline perturbation (amino-acid removal) is implementable within the validated envelope; if mid-run AA removal is not supported, we substitute a static amino-acid-limited condition or accept this as a scope limitation.
The relA knockout eliminates the primary ppGpp synthesis pathway; any residual ppGpp synthesis (e.g. via SpoT) is negligible or the model accounts for it.

The ppgpp_conc perturbation with multiplier=1.0 clamps ppGpp concentration to 1× the basal (unstressed) level, preventing the starvation-induced spike in ppGpp. If multiplier=1.0 is instead a no-op (allowing normal dynamics), the rival #2 test does not distinguish causality and this design must be reinterpreted or replaced.

hyp_agent.h1: Stringent-response activation (elevated ppGpp) causally reduces growth rate and ribosome concentration, creating a transient phenotypic tolerance to growth-dependent antibiotics without genetic resistance mutations.
hyp_agent.h0: Growth rate and ribosome concentration are independent of ppGpp level; antibiotic survival requires genetic resistance mutations.

rivals.claim: Rival 1 (Metabolic-shutdown hypothesis): Growth arrest is driven by direct metabolic collapse (e.g. ATP depletion, redox imbalance) rather than ppGpp-mediated transcriptional reallocation. ppGpp is a correlate, not a cause.

Antibiotic susceptibility correlates with growth_rate and ribosome_conc in vivo; the simulator does not model antibiotic action directly, so we test the physiological preconditions (growth arrest, ribosome reduction) that confer tolerance.
The persister phenotype is operationalized as a low-growth, low-ribosome state; we do not distinguish deterministic bistability from stochastic entry into this state within the scope of this test.
